sub_tasks.requirements: Method and code fidelity to the paper

The RLM scaffold implements Algorithm 1 exactly: initializes a Python REPL with the user prompt P bound to a variable, exposes a sub_RLM function, and runs an iteration loop in which the root LLM only ever sees constant-size metadata (length, short prefix) about P and about printed stdout — the full prompt text is never appended to the root model's history.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: Termination is implemented via setting a `Final` variable inside the REPL (as specified in Algorithm 1), and the value bound to `Final` is returned as the response Y; there is no `Finish`/answer action that asks the model to verbalize the output directly.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: Sub-call recursion is symbolic and programmatic: the REPL exposes the sub-RLM/sub-LM as a callable module so the root can launch sub-calls inside Python loops over slices of P (enabling Ω(|P|) and Ω(|P|^2) sub-call counts), not as a single verbalized tool action per turn.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: A configurable max recursion depth parameter is implemented with the paper's semantics: depth=0 disables sub-calls, depth=1 enables sub-LM (non-recursive) sub-calls, and depth≥2 enables sub-RLM calls — matching the RLM(model, depth=N) notation in §3.2.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: stdout from REPL execution is truncated/summarized to a short metadata prefix before being appended to the root model's history, as described in Algorithm 1 footnote 2, to prevent context overflow.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: The root model is driven by the system prompt described in Appendix C of the paper (or its fine-tuned equivalent for RLM-Qwen3-8B per Appendix A), instructing it to probe P, decompose, and call sub-RLMs over slices.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: Data and preprocessing fidelity
S-NIAH is constructed as 50 single-needle tasks from RULER with input lengths swept from 2^13 to 2^20 tokens, matching the protocol used in Figure 1.

BrowseComp-Plus (1K) uses 150 randomly sampled queries from BrowseComp-Plus with 1000 documents per instance (gold + evidence guaranteed present), following Sun et al. 2025 — not the full corpus and not a different sample size.

OOLONG uses the trec_coarse split with the 50-task evaluation set as specified in §3.1, and OOLONG-Pairs uses the 20 paper-specified pairwise queries listed in Appendix D.1 over the same trec_coarse data.
LongBench-v2 CodeQA evaluation uses the CodeQA multi-choice split of LongBench-v2 with task lengths spanning the 23K–4.2M token range reported in Table 1.

For RLM-Qwen3-8B fine-tuning, the training set is exactly 1,000 filtered RLM(Qwen3-Coder-480B-A35B) trajectories on LongBenchPro tasks, with sub-calls made by Qwen3-8B, as described in §3.2 and Appendix A.
Experiment execution and reproducibility
The GPT-5 RLM configuration is executed with GPT-5 (medium reasoning, default sampling) as the root and GPT-5-mini as the sub-call/recursive model, matching the split used in §3.2.
The Qwen3-Coder-480B-A35B RLM configuration runs end-to-end using the sampling parameters specified by Qwen Team 2025b, and costs are computed against the Fireworks provider pricing as in Table 1.

Required baselines are implemented and executed against the same instances: CodeAct+BM25, CodeAct+sub-calls, the Compaction agent (with GPT-5-nano compactor + GPT-5 final answer for the GPT-5 row), OpenCode (with and without context offloading), and Claude Code v2.0.0 + Claude Opus 4.1 (with and without context offloading).
All four RLM recursion depths (0, 1, 2, 3) are run for at least one of GPT-5 or Qwen3-Coder-480B-A35B and reported in the results table, enabling the depth-ablation observations in §4.
The length-scaling sweep from 2^13 to 2^20 tokens is executed for both base GPT-5 and RLM(GPT-5, depth=1) on S-NIAH, OOLONG, and OOLONG-Pairs, as required to reproduce Figure 1.
Evaluation protocol and metric correctness
OOLONG-Pairs is scored with F1 over the list-of-entries answer (not exact-match accuracy), as specified in §3.1.
OOLONG, BrowseComp-Plus, and S-NIAH are scored as percent-correct over their respective evaluation sets (50/150/50 tasks); LongBench-v2 CodeQA is scored as multi-choice accuracy.

Per-method API cost is computed and reported as mean ± standard deviation per task using the provider-specific pricing (OpenAI for GPT-5 family, Fireworks for Qwen3, Anthropic for Claude Opus 4.1), matching the gray-text cost cells in Table 1.
Runs that hit input-context limits are marked with an asterisk (*) in the reported table, and methods for which cost is not applicable are reported as N/A, mirroring the paper's reporting convention.
Syntax-error and first-decomposition categorization on RLM(depth=1) trajectories is computed for Figure 4(b), bucketed by correct vs. incorrect rollouts.
Result match versus the paper's reported targets
RLM(GPT-5, depth=1) reproduces the headline Table 1 numbers within a reasonable tolerance: CodeQA 62.0, BrowseComp+ (1K) 91.3, OOLONG 56.0, OOLONG-Pairs 58.0.
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: Base GPT-5 vs RLM(GPT-5) gap is reproduced on the two information-dense tasks: base GPT-5 ≤ ~0.1 F1 on OOLONG-Pairs while RLM(GPT-5, depth=1) reaches ~58% F1, and OOLONG improves from ~44% to ~56%.
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: Depth scaling on OOLONG-Pairs with GPT-5 shows monotonic improvement across depths (~43.9 → 58.0 → 65.5 → 76.0 for depths 0→1→2→3), reproducing the trend in Table 1.
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: Figure 1 is reproduced: base GPT-5 shows steep degradation as length grows on OOLONG and OOLONG-Pairs while RLM(GPT-5, depth=1) maintains strong performance across 2^13 to 2^20 tokens, including handling lengths beyond GPT-5's 272K context window.
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: RLM-Qwen3-8B outperforms the Qwen3-8B base-as-RLM on all four Table 1 benchmarks with a median improvement of approximately 28% (Figure 3a).
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: Artifact completeness and provenance

The repository contains a reproducible entry point that exposes the RLM as a string-in/string-out callable (matching the external interface described in §2) and includes the system prompts used (Appendix C, including the LongCoT decomposition-hints variant from Appendix C.3 if Table 2 is targeted).

Final report / results artifact records per-task scores, per-task mean and std cost, recursion depth, and the underlying root and sub-call model identifiers (e.g., GPT-5 + GPT-5-mini, or Qwen3-Coder-480B-A35B + Qwen3-Coder-480B-A35B), so each row of Table 1 is traceable to a concrete run.
Released artifacts include either the fine-tuned RLM-Qwen3-8B checkpoint (or its training script + the 1,000 filtered LongBenchPro trajectories) sufficient to reproduce Figure 3(a).
